pattern: A value only ever competes against itself: sitting away from the two ends of the array already puts an occurrence under more than one window on its own, so only whatever sits at the very first or very last position can possibly be almost missing -- the whole problem reduces to three tiny cases.
hints: When the window is a single element, 'appears in exactly one window' just means that value shows up exactly once in the whole array.
When the window is the entire array, there is only one window total, so every distinct value present automatically qualifies -- the answer is simply the largest value anywhere in nums.

For any window size strictly between those two extremes, an occurrence away from the two ends is always covered by more than one window, so only the first and last elements can ever qualify -- check whether each occurs exactly once anywhere in the array, and answer with the larger qualifying one (or -1 if neither does).

edge_inputs.description: statement example, a repeated value that still qualifies at one end
window of size one, all values distinct
window of size one, one value duplicated
window equal to the whole array, duplicates present
the same duplicated value sits at both ends, must disqualify both
single element array, answer must be 0 rather than the -1 sentinel
two identical elements with a size-one window, both disqualify each other

stress.description: random array of small values with a random window size across the full length range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 50))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 50) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [nums, k]
